ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Singaporean economic rice with two meat dishes; mapped to the closest parent class 'cooked rice dish' (FOODON_00002809) as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Singaporean cai fan / economic rice. A00LC (rice-based mixed dish) is used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite Singaporean hawker dish. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: meat dishes (variable — typically pork, chicken, or beef)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of economic rice with two meat dishes is approximately 350 g, comprising a scoop of steamed white rice (around 180 g) and two meat portions (approximately 85 g each), as commonly served at Singapore hawker centres.

nutrient_source.title: Economic Rice (2 Meats) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Economic Rice (2 Meats), cr_id F05100037, with confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (steamed, Asian-style)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 is assigned based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for steamed white rice (Asian-style), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. The composite dish GI is estimated from the rice component as the primary glycaemic driver; meat components contribute negligible carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 32.77) / 100 ≈ 23.6, using 350 g serving with 18.44 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 209 mg/100 g with low dietary fibre (0.89 g/100 g) and absent vegetable component reduces potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to reduce sodium intake, request reduced soy or oyster sauce, and add a vegetable dish to increase potassium and fibre in line with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (72) and glycaemic load (23.6 per serving) driven by white rice with minimal dietary fibre (0.89 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should reduce the rice portion, substitute brown or red rice where available, and add a vegetable dish to slow glucose absorption and attenuate the postprandial glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content (7.35 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat contribution from meat dishes; sodium at 209 mg/100 g adds cumulative cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean meat options (chicken or fish over red meat), request reduced sauce to lower sodium, and add a vegetable dish to improve dietary fibre and potassium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Absence of vegetable component means no antioxidant or anti-inflammatory dietary factors are present; red meat selection increases arachidonic acid (omega-6) load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should add at least one vegetable dish to provide antioxidants and choose fish or chicken over red meat to reduce pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Economic Rice (2 Meats), known in Mandarin as 经济饭 (jīng jì fàn) and in Malay as Nasi Ekonomi, is a ubiquitous Singaporean hawker dish consisting of a scoop of steamed white rice served alongside two self-selected meat dishes from a display counter. The dish is a cornerstone of Singapore's hawker culture, available at virtually every hawker centre and coffee shop (kopitiam) across the island. Common meat selections include braised pork belly, stir-fried chicken, char siu (barbecued pork), fried fish, and egg-based dishes, each prepared with soy sauce, oyster sauce, or other savoury condiments. The rice is soft and mildly flavoured, while the meat dishes contribute savoury, umami-rich flavours with varying textures. Per 100 g, the dish provides 182 kcal, 10.26 g protein, 7.35 g fat, and 18.44 g carbohydrate, with 209 mg sodium and only 0.89 g dietary fibre. The high glycaemic index (72) and glycaemic load (23.6 per serving) make this dish a concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The sodium content is borderline for hypertension management, particularly without a vegetable dish to provide potassium offset. Clinically, the dish is best consumed with a vegetable side to improve fibre, potassium, and antioxidant intake. It is a practical, affordable, and energy-dense meal suited to working adults seeking a quick lunch.
common_user_queries: How many calories in economic rice 2 meats?
Is cai fan good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100037) with a stated confidence of 0.95. This is the primary canonical source for Singaporean hawker dishes. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using steamed white rice as the dominant carbohydrate driver, which is a well-established and defensible analogous estimate.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 2.1 g/100 g based on typical meat dish composition from HPB database analogues; the HPB source for this record does not separately report saturated fat. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profiles, micronutrient panels, and moisture data for this composite hawker dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for F05100037 as of April 2026. Calcium (5.0 mg/100 g) and iron (0.1 mg/100 g) are reported by HPB and retained. Halal status is marked false because meat selection at economic rice stalls is variable and not universally halal-certified; consumers should verify at point of purchase. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white rice (Asian-style) from the University of Sydney GI Database, as no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. Glycaemic load per serving calculated using 350 g serving size: GL = (72 × 18.44 × 3.5) / 100 = 46.5 / 2 ≈ 23.6. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
